video_title: AMD, Starcloud, Coatue..10 Hot Takes From The Biggest Names in AI
gist: A rapid-fire hallway-interview reel from the Raise Summit in Europe: Coatue's Max Cook argues the keyboard/mouse era is ending in favor of natural-language agent interfaces; AMD's CTO, Factory's CEO, and Decagon data all point to open-source models rapidly taking enterprise token share; SambaNova announces a $1B raise at an $11B valuation; and StarCloud, Black Forest Labs, and Liquid AI make the case for space-based compute, open innovation, and efficiency-first foundation models respectively.

insights.claim: Coatue's Max Cook argues consumers are shifting from having 60-80 apps on their phone (of which they use only ~11 daily, ~30 monthly) to a compressed agentic world of just 2-4 apps that plug into everything else via API or MCP — a shift he says will force a parallel move away from keyboard-and-mouse toward natural-language interaction as the primary compute interface.
insights.speaker: Max Cook, Coatue
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-interface debate: the app-consolidation trend (fewer, agent-orchestrated apps) is the leading indicator for why voice/natural-language UX investment should accelerate now.
insights.quote: the average person has 60 to 80 apps on their phone

insights.claim: Cook cites OpenAI's forthcoming consumer 'super app' — reaching nearly a billion weekly active users — as the catalyst event that will force a broad redesign of how people interact with agents on their phones.
insights.speaker: Max Cook, Coatue
insights.so_what: Flags OpenAI's consumer super-app launch as a near-term forcing function for interface/UX investment across the AI app layer.
insights.quote: OpenAI coming out with a super app for the first time that's going to introduce their almost a billion weekly active users to agents

insights.claim: Cook says Thinking Machines' May interaction-model demo (near-zero-latency, multimodal voice/image back-and-forth) was underhyped relative to its importance, calling it a preview of the interface layer he expects to eventually sit on top of every model and app.
insights.speaker: Max Cook, Coatue
insights.so_what: Points investors toward interaction-model/voice-latency infrastructure as an underrated layer of the AI stack worth tracking, separate from the model-provider race itself.

themes.theme: Open vs. Closed Models — Real Enterprise Token-Share Data

insights.claim: Cook cites Decagon founder Jesse Zhang's claim that roughly 90% of Decagon's production workloads run on open-source models for latency and fine-tuning reasons, even though open-source's overall share of enterprise LLM spend fell from 19% to 11% year over year — implying open-source wins once a workflow reaches production scale while frontier closed models win for discovering new use cases.
insights.speaker: Max Cook, Coatue
insights.so_what: Suggests the open-vs-closed debate is a false binary tied to workflow maturity: expect open source to keep winning high-volume production workloads even as closed-model frontier spend share falls at the aggregate enterprise level.
insights.quote: I think 90% of Decagon's workloads run on open source models

insights.claim: Factory's CEO says enterprise adoption of open-source models grew from under 1% at the start of the year, crossing 1% in March and 10% in May, and predicts it will cross 50% of enterprise tokens by the end of the year — arguing frontier labs have been spreading 'propaganda' against Chinese open models despite their strong performance, cost, and speed.
insights.speaker: Matan, Factory
insights.so_what: A specific, trackable forecast (50% of enterprise tokens open-source by year-end) that investors can check against future surveys to gauge how fast frontier-model pricing power is eroding.
insights.quote: in the next 12 months 90% of tokens will be going to open models

insights.claim: Factory's CEO says his platform dynamically routes enterprise tasks to whichever open or cheaper model fits, and expects enterprise open-model usage to cross the 50% threshold by year-end.
insights.speaker: Matan, Factory
insights.so_what: Reinforces that model routing (not a single frontier model) is becoming the default enterprise architecture, compressing how much revenue any single closed-model provider can capture per task.
insights.quote: we'll probably cross the 50% threshold by the end of this calendar year

insights.claim: Cook raises a question he says the open/closed debate is missing: whether Anthropic and OpenAI will start selling their own older, cheaper models as fine-tunable/routable products, which would blur the open-versus-closed dividing line entirely.
insights.speaker: Max Cook, Coatue
insights.so_what: Flags a possible strategic move (frontier labs commercializing their own legacy models for the routing/fine-tuning market) that would undercut third-party open-source providers' cost advantage.

insights.claim: AMD's CTO says agentic AI workflows are pushing the CPU-to-GPU deployment ratio toward roughly 1:1, a pace of adoption over the last six months he says caught even AMD off guard.
insights.speaker: Mark, CTO of AMD
insights.so_what: A concrete infrastructure data point (near-parity CPU:GPU ratio) suggesting agentic workloads are more CPU-intensive than the pure-GPU-scaling narrative implies — relevant for sizing AMD's addressable market versus GPU-only competitors.
insights.quote: the ratio of CPU to GPU is becoming like one to one.

insights.claim: AMD says it now uses 'thousands of sub-agents' internally to speed up chip design cycles, citing it as a concrete production use case for agentic AI beyond typical software-engineering copilots.
insights.speaker: Mark, CTO of AMD
insights.so_what: A real-world example of agentic AI compressing hardware R&D cycle time — relevant to how fast chip design/iteration speed could accelerate industry-wide.

insights.claim: SambaNova announced a $1 billion Series F at an $11 billion valuation, led by General Atlantic with participation from T. Rowe Price and Capital Group, earmarked to scale the supply chain for delivering its inference racks; it is also about to ship a new chip (SN50) positioned for 'premium inference' — running large models at full precision at higher throughput and lower cost/power than GPUs, aimed at raising service providers' margins.
insights.speaker: Rodrigo, SambaNova
insights.so_what: A concrete new inference-chip competitor and funding data point (SambaNova's $11B valuation, SN50 chip) for investors tracking alternatives to Nvidia GPUs in the inference market.
insights.quote: We're announcing the first close or a series F is a $1 billion uh raise at 11 billion valuation.

insights.claim: StarCloud's next-generation orbital data-center vehicle ('StarCloud 3'), sized to fit inside a single Starship launch, is designed to carry up to 10 megawatts of compute capacity per launch; the company is moving into a much larger manufacturing campus in August to scale production.
insights.speaker: Philip Johnston, StarCloud
insights.so_what: A concrete capacity benchmark (10MW of compute per Starship launch) for gauging how fast space-based data centers could scale as a compute source once Starship cadence increases.
insights.quote: we can potentially fit up to 10 megawatts of compute capacity per Starship launch.

insights.claim: Johnston predicts SpaceX's eventual IPO will be viewed historically as 'the most undervalued IPO of all time,' expecting the company to tear through a $10 trillion valuation within a few years given its dominant low-cost launch position — comparing it to owning the railroads that every downstream space business (asteroid mining, lunar mining, orbital comms, and StarCloud itself) will have to build on top of.
insights.speaker: Philip Johnston, StarCloud
insights.so_what: A specific, checkable valuation call ($10T within a few years) on SpaceX, framed around its launch-cost moat as the foundational infrastructure layer for the entire space economy.
insights.quote: the SpaceX IPO is the going to be viewed historically as the most undervalued IPO of all time.

insights.claim: StarCloud says it is chip-agnostic for its orbital compute payloads, planning to fly Nvidia, Cerebras, and SambaNova chips, and notes it already flew ARM GPUs on its first spacecraft.
insights.speaker: Philip Johnston, StarCloud
insights.so_what: Signals that no single chip vendor has locked up the emerging space-compute market — a multi-vendor opportunity rather than an Nvidia-exclusive one.

insights.claim: Black Forest Labs' Robin argues that AI fear-mongering pushes labs toward closed models, which he believes ultimately slows AI progress and safety, making the case that 'open innovation is good for the world' regardless of whether it takes the form of open weights or full open source.
insights.speaker: Robin, Black Forest Labs
insights.so_what: A direct counter-argument to the safety-driven case for closed model weights, from a builder of multimodal visual/physical-AI models.
insights.quote: open innovation is good for the world.

insights.claim: Robin says Europe's AI/tech ecosystem is still nascent relative to its cultural reputation ('not known for having nice technology'), but is inflecting as more industry events and investment converge on the region.
insights.speaker: Robin, Black Forest Labs
insights.so_what: A grounded, insider read on Europe's AI competitiveness gap versus the US/China — useful context for evaluating European AI startup valuations and hype.

insights.claim: Liquid AI's Reine proposes reframing foundation-model strategy as a three-axis optimization — capability/intelligence, cost/efficiency, and deployment substrate (data center vs. edge devices like phones, cars, and laptops) — rather than a single-axis race to maximize intelligence, arguing energy and chip-supply constraints make efficiency a 'first-class citizen,' not an afterthought.
insights.speaker: Reine, Liquid AI
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete evaluation framework for foundation-model companies beyond pure benchmark performance — investors should weight cost-efficiency and deployability alongside intelligence when assessing model-company moats.

insights.claim: Liquid AI is already deploying its efficient foundation models inside Mercedes-Benz vehicles for in-car intelligence, framed as an example of pushing AI onto edge 'substrates' (Raspberry-Pi-class devices) rather than only running it in data centers.
insights.speaker: Reine, Liquid AI
insights.so_what: A concrete production deployment (automotive OEM) validating small, efficient on-device foundation models as a viable commercial category distinct from cloud-hosted frontier models.

insights.claim: Good Future Media's Chris says professional short-form clipping of long-form podcast/interview content has become a distinct paid service (around $200 per clip minimum, with clients ideally publishing 1-3 clips daily) because human editorial judgment on hooks and pacing still outperforms fully-automated AI clipping tools.
insights.speaker: Chris, Good Future Media
insights.so_what: A concrete pricing benchmark ($200/clip) for the human-in-the-loop content-distribution layer that's grown up around the podcast/interview boom — a durable services business even as AI clipping tools proliferate.
insights.quote: 200 bucks a clip is pretty standard in our agency.

insights.claim: General Intuition's Pim frames most of current AI progress as 'glorified data science' — driven more by high-quality, well-curated datasets than by architecture innovation — while declining to disclose where or how the company sources and stores its training data.
insights.speaker: Pim, General Intuition
insights.so_what: A reminder from a model-training practitioner that data curation, not model architecture, remains the primary bottleneck and competitive moat in current AI development.
